actions.thought: In Heads-Up No-Limit Hold'em with 100BB stacks, the Button (Small Blind) usually opens a wide range (around 80-90% of hands). However, 6c 7h (76o) sits near the bottom of the playable range. In a 'Raise or Fold' strategy, 76o is typically a fold as it lacks high-card strength and dominance, and cannot call a 3-bet. While a limping strategy is viable, starting the match conservatively by folding marginal off-suit connectors minimizes early variance and avoids difficult post-flop spots with weak holdings. I will concede the small blind.

Holding 8s 3c in the Small Blind is a very weak starting hand. While I have the option to complete the blind for 50 or raise to steal the pot, 83 offsuit has extremely poor playability and realization of equity post-flop. Against a competent opponent ('Pro'), it is generally better to fold these bottom-tier hands and wait for better spots rather than bloating a pot out of position or with a garbage hand.

In Heads-Up play, being on the Button (Small Blind) provides a positional advantage. With K5o, I hold a King, which acts as a decent blocker and has reasonable high-card value against a random hand. While K5o is on the lower end of the playable range, folding is too passive given the width of heads-up ranges (usually opening 80%+ of buttons). A min-raise allows me to seize the initiative and potentially take down the pot pre-flop or play with the betting lead in position. Limping is an alternative, but raising puts more pressure on the opponent. I will raise to 200.

I am in the Big Blind with 8h 7c. Pro has raised to 200 from the Button. I only need to call 100 more to see the flop in a pot of 300, giving me 4:1 pot odds. 87o is a standard defending hand in heads-up play as it has reasonable equity and playability on many board textures. I will call to see the flop and evaluate from there.

The flop is 6d 3d 6s. I have 8-high with very little equity on this board. Since I defended my Big Blind pre-flop, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. I will check and see if Pro decides to continuation bet or check back.

On a paired board of [6d, 3d, 6s], the Big Blind defender has a range advantage regarding trips, though they possess many missed hands. I hold [Kd, 5h], giving me a King-high flush draw and two overcards to the 3. While a small continuation bet is a valid high-frequency play heads-up to deny equity, checking back is superior here. It guarantees I realize my equity by seeing the turn for free, avoids the disaster of facing a check-raise, and strengthens my checking range. Additionally, King-high has some showdown value against missed broadway hands. I will take the free card.

I turned top pair on a 6-3-6-8 board with three diamonds. Pro checked back on the flop, which often suggests they don't have a 6 or a strong overpair. By betting now, I can extract value from weaker pairs, draws, or Ace-high hands that might call. I also protect my hand against various river cards.

The board is [6d, 3d, 6s, 8d]. I hold [Kd, 5h]. With three diamonds on the board and the King of diamonds in my hand, I have a made King-high flush. The board is paired, introducing a risk of Full Houses (if opponent holds 68, 88, 33, or 63), and the Ace-high flush beats me. However, in a Heads-Up scenario, my hand is extremely strong. The opponent has led out (donk bet) on the turn after I checked back the flop. This lead could be a bluff, a 6 (trips), or a worse flush. Raising here isolates me against hands that beat me (boats, nut flush) and folds out bluffs. Calling allows me to keep the opponent's bluffing range wide, avoids getting blown off my equity if they 3-bet, and positions me to call a river bet or bet for value in position on the river. I am way ahead of their range, so slow-playing the turn is optimal.

I have a pair of eights on a board with a pair of sixes and three diamonds. The river King is an overcard to my pair. After Pro checked back the flop and called my turn bet, their range could include missed flush draws, weaker pairs, or perhaps a King that they just hit. My hand has some showdown value, but it is unlikely to get called by many worse hands if I bet. Checking is the safest way to reach showdown or evaluate if Pro bets.

On the river, the board is [6d, 3d, 6s, 8d, Ks]. I hold [Kd, 5h] giving me a King-high flush. The opponent led the turn for 250 and then checked the river. This line often indicates a capped range, such as trips (6x), a medium flush, or top pair (Kx) trying to get to showdown cheaply. Checking back is too passive given the strength of my hand; I beat everything except Full Houses and the Ace-high flush. Heads-up ranges are wide, so it is unlikely the opponent is trapping with the absolute nuts. I will bet for value, targeting trips and worse flushes. A half-pot bet of 450 is large enough to extract value but small enough to get called by worse hands.

I have two pair, Eights and Sixes, with a King kicker. The betting sequence is telling: Pro checked back on the flop, which usually rules out very strong hands like a Six or a high overpair. Pro then called my lead on the turn when the third diamond and an Eight landed. On the river King, I checked to evaluate, and Pro bet 450 into 900. This 1/2 pot bet could represent a King that caught up, a flush, or a bluff with missed draws (like diamonds). Given the 3:1 pot odds and the fact that Pro's flop check capped their range, my hand is strong enough to call here to catch potential bluffs or missed draws.

In heads-up play, Ace-Four offsuit (A4o) is a strong starting hand, significantly ahead of a random range. Being on the button (Small Blind), I want to play aggressively to seize the initiative and build a pot or win the blinds immediately. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here.

I hold 7s 2s in the Big Blind. My opponent, Flash, has raised to 300 (3bb) from the Button. 72s is statistically one of the weakest hands in poker. Although I am getting 2:1 pot odds, playing this hand Out of Position against a larger-than-minimum raise is not profitable. I need roughly 33% equity to call, and against a reasonable opening range, 72s struggles to realize that equity, especially without initiative and position. Folding is the disciplined choice.

I am on the Button/SB with J4o. In Heads-Up play, while ranges are wide, J4o is near the bottom of the range and has poor playability due to being unsuited and disconnected. With 100bb deep stacks, playing this hand often leads to difficult post-flop situations or bleeding chips. Standard equilibrium charts suggest folding this specific hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I have been dealt pocket Aces, the strongest possible starting hand in Texas Hold'em. My objective is to build value and maximize the pot while I have a significant mathematical advantage. I am on the button (Small Blind), and I will put in a standard raise to 300 chips to build the pot and potentially induce a re-raise from the Big Blind.

I am in the Big Blind with 84o, facing a 3bb (300) raise from the Button. In heads-up play, while ranges are wide, 84o is near the bottom of my range and performs poorly against a standard opening range, especially out of position. The opponent's larger sizing (3x) worsens my pot odds compared to a min-raise, requiring stronger holdings to defend. My raw equity is likely around 33%, which is barely break-even, but my equity realization will be poor due to the lack of connectivity and suitedness. Folding is the most prudent decision.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Three offsuit (T3o). In Heads-Up play, while ranges are wide, T3o is near the bottom of the starting hand distribution (bottom 15-20%). It has poor playability due to being unconnected and unsuited with a weak kicker. Although I have position post-flop, playing this hand likely yields a negative expectation, especially against an opponent who might defend or raise. Folding is the standard decision for this specific hand to preserve chips.

I am on the Button with 5d 2s, which is one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em. While the opponent (Pro) has been playing relatively tight and folding often pre-flop, this specific hand is too weak to justify a raise or a call, even with positional advantage post-flop. I will preserve my stack and wait for a better opportunity to exploit the opponent's tendencies.

I am in the Small Blind holding 4d 3c, which is 4-3 offsuit. This is one of the weakest starting hands in Heads-Up No-Limit Hold'em, sitting at the very bottom of my range. It has poor playability due to being unsuited and disconnected enough to make straights difficult, with very little showdown value. Although I have folded my button frequently in recent hands, forcing action with this specific hand is unnecessary and negative EV. The standard play is to simply fold.

In heads-up play, the Button (Small Blind) has position post-flop. 3s 6s is a suited hand that, while weak, is well within the standard opening range for a 2-player game. I have successfully stolen the blinds in previous hands (Hand 4 and 6) with a raise to 300. I will continue this aggressive strategy to take control of the pot early.

I am in the Big Blind with Q4o facing a 3x raise (300) from the Button. The opponent has been opening this size frequently. While I get 3:1 pot odds, Q4o is a weak hand that plays poorly post-flop out of position, often dominated or difficult to realize equity. Against a 3x open, this is a standard fold.

I am on the Button (Small Blind) with K9o, which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play (well above the median). I have folded several buttons recently due to poor cards (Hands 1, 5, 7, 9), so my image might appear tight. I want to seize the initiative and utilize my position. A min-raise to 200 is a standard opening size that risks little while putting pressure on the Big Blind to defend. Folding is too passive for this hand strength, and limping allows the BB to see a free flop with random holdings.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Eight offsuit (K8o). The opponent in the Small Blind/Button has raised to 200, requiring me to call an additional 100 chips. With pot odds of 3:1 and a hand that has decent high-card value and playability in heads-up pots, this is a standard defend. I will call to see a flop and evaluate my hand's strength based on the board texture.

I have hit middle pair with a King kicker on a board of Jack-Eight-Four. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard play is to check to them. My hand is strong enough to call a continuation bet, but leading out (donk betting) here would likely only get called by better hands or folds from worse. Checking allows me to see how the opponent reacts to the board texture.

I hold King-high (Ks 9c) on a wet flop of [4c 8c Jh]. As the pre-flop raiser, I have a range advantage, but this board texture interacts well with the Big Blind's calling range (flush draws, straight draws, pairs like Jx, 8x, 4x). While a c-bet is standard, my hand has some showdown value and limited playability if raised. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, control the size of the pot, and potentially hit a King, Nine, or club on the turn. I avoid getting blown off my equity by a check-raise.

On a board of 4c-8c-Jh-Js, I have two pair (Jacks and Eights) with a King kicker. The flop went check-check, which suggests the opponent likely does not have a Jack or a very strong hand, though they could be trapping. My Eight is currently a strong hand in a checked-through pot. I will lead with a 'probe bet' of 200 (half pot) to extract value from draws (like the club flush draw), lower pairs, or Ace-high hands that might call one street. This also helps define the strength of the opponent's hand.

I checked back the flop with King-high to pot control, and the turn pairs the board with the Jack of Spades. The opponent's lead of 200 (half-pot) after the flop went check-check is frequently a probe bet with a wide range, including semi-bluffs (flush draws, straight draws) or weak pairs, trying to capitalize on my perceived weakness. The board pairing reduces the likelihood of him holding a Jack. Getting 3:1 odds, my hand has sufficient showdown value against his bluffs, and I have 6 outs (Kings and Nines) if I am currently behind a smaller pair like 8x or 4x. Folding would be too exploitable in this heads-up dynamic. Calling allows me to keep his bluffing range in and re-evaluate on the river.

On this river, my hand has improved to a full house, Eights full of Jacks (8-8-8-J-J). While this is a very strong hand, the board texture is dangerous. Any Jack in my opponent's hand (which is highly likely given they raised pre-flop and called my turn bet) now has a superior full house, Jacks full of Eights (J-J-J-8-8). By checking, I am exercising pot control. If my opponent has the Jack, I minimize my losses, as I can call a standard-sized bet but would be wary of a massive raise. If my opponent was on a draw (like clubs) or has a smaller pocket pair, checking may induce a bluff or allow them to check behind with a hand that would have folded to a bet anyway.

The river is the 8 of Spades, putting a second pair on the board [4c 8c Jh Js 8s]. My hand is now Two Pair, Jacks and Eights, with a King kicker (JJ88K). This is a strong showdown hand because the double-paired board counterfeits any pocket pair lower than Eights (e.g., 66 plays as JJ886, which loses to my JJ88K) and beats any non-Ace high card hand (e.g., missed flush draws like QcTc). Betting is unlikely to extract value from worse hands, as they will simply fold, and better hands like Ace-high or a full house will call or raise. Checking back guarantees I realize my equity against his bluffs and counterfeited pairs without risking chips against his value range.

I am on the button with 7s 8c, which is a standard opening hand in heads-up play at 100BB depth. My opponent, Pro, has folded to several of my pre-flop raises in earlier hands (Hands 4, 6, and 10), suggesting they are playing a somewhat disciplined or tight defense. I will raise to 300 to exert pressure and take advantage of my positional advantage post-flop if called.

I hold 7h 4h in the Big Blind facing a 3x raise (300) from the Button. While suited hands have some playability, 74s is a weak two-gapper. Being out of position against a larger-than-minimum raise requires stronger holdings to call profitably, as realizing equity will be difficult. The pot odds (2:1) require ~33% equity, which this hand barely meets raw, but realization is the issue. I have folded to his 300 raises previously with junk, and this hand is still too marginal to defend OOP against this sizing.

I hold 6c 8h in the Small Blind. This is a weak off-suit connector (8-6 offsuit). In a heads-up match, while ranges are wider, this hand falls toward the bottom of the playable range. I am out of position against the Big Blind. My previous play in the SB with similar weak hands (like 6c 7h in Hand 1) has been to fold. The opponent has shown aggression when in position. Playing this hand OOP is likely -EV. Folding preserves my stack for better opportunities.

I'm holding Ace-Ten offsuit on the button, which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play. My opponent has folded to several 3x opens recently. I will raise to 300 to either take the blinds or build a pot with a hand that has good high-card value and equity.

I am in the Big Blind facing a 3bb (300) raise from the Button. My hand is J5o, which is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability. The opponent has been consistently opening to 300. The pot odds require me to call 200 to win a total pot of 600 (33% equity required). While J5o has some raw equity against a wide range, it suffers from domination and realizes equity poorly out of position. Against a larger 3x sizing, J5o is a standard fold. I will wait for a better hand to defend.

I hold J9o on the Button (SB) in a heads-up match. This hand is well within the top 50% of hands and is a standard open. My opponent (Flash) has shown a tendency to defend the Big Blind against min-raises (Hands #3, #11), but maintaining the initiative and position is crucial. A min-raise to 200 is the most balanced approach to build the pot while keeping risk low, allowing me to capitalize on position post-flop.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Seven of hearts. The opponent (Small Blind) has raised to 200. I need to call 100 more to see the flop. With suited connectors/one-gappers like Q7h, I have good pot odds (3:1) and reasonable playability post-flop to defend my big blind. I will call to see the flop.

On this Jd-5c-6d flop, I have missed completely with my Qh7h. As the Big Blind defending against a pre-flop raise, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. My hand has very little equity, but checking allows me to see how the opponent reacts without committing more chips immediately. I will check and likely fold to a significant bet unless I pick up equity on the turn.

I have Top Pair (Jacks) with a Nine kicker on a Jd 5c 6d board. While this is a strong hand in heads-up play, the board is somewhat wet with flush and straight draws. Reviewing previous hands (Hand #3 and #11), my opponent 'Flash' has a specific tendency: when I raise pre-flop and check back the flop, Flash leads out (bets) on the turn. In those previous hands, I had weak holdings, but here I have a strong value hand. By checking back now, I accomplish two things: 1) I control the pot size with a marginal top pair, avoiding a difficult spot if check-raised, and 2) I exploit Flash's tendency to bluff or stab at the pot on the turn when he perceives weakness. This allows me to extract value from his bluffs or weaker hands on later streets.

I am first to act on the turn. The board now has three diamonds (Jd, 6d, 9d) and I have no diamonds in my hand, meaning I have no flush or flush draw. I do have a gutshot straight draw (needing an 8), but the 8 of diamonds would be dangerous. Since I missed the flop and the turn card is a scare card that likely favors the pre-flop aggressor's range or completes a flush, I will check to see how the opponent reacts. Betting here as a bluff is risky without a diamond blocker.

On the Flop, I checked back Top Pair for pot control and to induce bluffs. The Turn 9d improves my hand to Top Two Pair (Jacks and Nines), but it is also a dangerous card that completes the front-door flush draw and connects straight draws. My opponent (Flash) checked the turn, deviating from his previous tendency to lead the turn after a flop check-back, which suggests he likely doesn't have a strong made hand or a pure air bluff, but rather a marginal hand or a draw he wants to see cheap. With Two Pair, I have significant value, but the board is very wet (3 diamonds). Checking back gives a free card to any single diamond or straight draw, which is risky. A bet serves for both value (targeting Jx, 6x, or 9x) and protection. I will bet half the pot (200) to charge draws and extract value from worse made hands without overcommitting against a possible flush.

On a board with three diamonds (Jd 5c 6d 9d), I have no diamonds in my hand, meaning I have no flush or flush draw. My hand is Queen-high with a weak gutshot straight draw to an 8. The opponent checked back the flop but has now bet half-pot on the turn. Since I have no showdown value and my straight draw is compromised by the flush possibility (even if I hit an 8, it might be the 8 of diamonds or I could already be against a flush), there is no reason to continue. I will fold.

I am on the button with Ace-Deuce suited (Ah 2h), which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. Standard strategy is to raise to gain initiative and build the pot. I have been raising to 300 in previous hands from the button, so I will maintain that sizing to remain consistent and put pressure on the Big Blind.

The opponent has opened to 3x (300) from the Button. While heads-up requires defending the Big Blind widely, a 3x raise offers significantly worse pot odds (33%) compared to a min-raise. J7o is a weak offsuit hand that plays poorly out of position and suffers from reverse implied odds. Despite the opponent's high opening frequency, J7o falls below the threshold for a profitable defense against this specific sizing due to the difficulty of realizing equity post-flop. Preserving chips for better spots is the prudent play.

I hold 5d 2c, which is statistically one of the weakest hands in Heads-Up No-Limit Hold'em. My opponent, Flash, has shown a tendency to defend their Big Blind by calling raises or checking options, meaning they are unlikely to fold often enough to make a bluff raise profitable with this holding. While I have position, the hand has negligible playability and equity. Folding is the standard and most positive Expected Value decision here to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with King-Queen offsuit, which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. My strategy so far has been to raise from the button to 300 when I have playable hands, as seen in previous hands like #4, #6, and #14. I will continue this pattern to maintain aggression and build the pot with a premium starting hand.

The opponent has been opening to 3x (300) frequently from the button. I hold AQo, a premium hand in Heads-Up play. While a 3-bet for value is a standard option, I have been folding to his raises consistently in previous hands (Hand #4, #6, #10, #12, #14, #16). A 3-bet now might look extremely strong and cause him to fold dominated hands (like Kx, Qx, Jx) that I want to keep in the pot. Calling allows me to keep his range wide, protects my calling range, and gives me the opportunity to outplay him post-flop or catch bluffs, given I am out of position. The pot odds are favorable to see a flop.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (TPTK) on a paired board (9c Qs 9d). As the pre-flop caller out of position, checking is the standard play. This board texture hits my defending range well, but leading out (donk betting) would likely fold out the opponent's bluffs and air, which constitutes a large part of his wide button opening range. By checking, I give the aggressive opponent an opportunity to make a continuation bet with a wide range, allowing me to extract value from bluffs or worse made hands later. I plan to call or potentially check-raise if he bets.

I have flopped top pair with a King kicker on a 9-Q-9 board, which is a very strong hand. My opponent (Pro) has checked. I want to bet for value here to build the pot, as Pro may call with hands like middle pairs, pocket pairs, or draws. A bet of 300, which is half the pot, is a standard size to get value while also providing some protection against potential straight draws.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (TPTK) on a paired board (9c Qs 9d). The opponent has bet half-pot (300 into 600). This board is relatively dry and static. If I raise now, I likely fold out all of the opponent's bluffs and air, which makes up a significant portion of his wide button opening range. I also risk isolating myself against a 9 (trips). By calling, I keep his range wide, allowing him to continue bluffing on the turn or value betting worse Queens. My hand is strong enough to call down multiple streets, but does not necessitate 'protection' via a raise on this texture.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (TPTK) with AsQc on a board of 9c Qs 9d Js. The turn card (Jack of Spades) coordinates the board significantly, completing potential straights for T8 and bringing a spade flush draw. While my hand remains strong relative to a wide button opening range, the board texture has become more dangerous. I am out of position. Checking is the standard line here to defer to the pre-flop aggressor who c-bet the flop. By checking, I induce bluffs from hands that missed or semi-bluffs from draws, and I avoid isolating myself against the top of his range (trips, straights, two pair) by leading out. If the opponent checks back, I can comfortably reassess on the river. If he bets, I will likely call to catch bluffs and value bet worse Queens, as folding TPTK in a heads-up match on this board is too tight without specific reads.

I have top pair with a strong kicker (Queens with a King). The Jack on the turn is a bit of a scare card as it completes some potential straights (T8) and could give the opponent two pair (QJ). However, Pro's check suggests they might still be holding a weaker hand or a draw. I will bet 600, which is half the pot, to extract value from weaker Queens, Jacks, or straight draws while also maintaining control of the hand. If I am raised, I will have to evaluate the strength of my top pair against a potentially superior hand like a nine or a straight.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (AsQc) on a board of 9c Qs 9d Js. The turn card complicates the board, bringing straight possibilities (T8) and a flush draw. The opponent has fired a second barrel of half-pot (600). In a Heads-Up match, TPTK is too strong to fold to a single turn bet, as the opponent's range remains wide, including semi-bluffs (flush draws, straight draws like KT) and worse value hands (KQ, QT). Raising would likely isolate me against hands that beat me (Trips, Straights, Two Pair) while folding out his bluffs. Calling is the best option to keep his range wide and reassess on the river.

I hold AsQc on a final board of 9c Qs 9d Js Jh. My hand is now Two Pair (Queens and Jacks) with an Ace kicker. The river double-pairs the board, making it highly coordinated. Any Nine or Jack in the opponent's hand makes a Full House, and KT/T8 makes a straight. Given the opponent's aggression on the flop and turn, checking is the correct play to pot control. Leading out would likely only get called by better hands (Full Houses, Straights) while folding out his bluffs. By checking, I allow him to bluff with missed draws or check back with worse showdown value (like KQ or underpairs). My hand has strong showdown value but is vulnerable to the specific runout, so I will check and evaluate his river action.

The board has double-paired with Jacks and Nines, and I hold a Queen, giving me two pair: Queens and Jacks with a King kicker. While this is a strong hand in heads-up play, the board texture is highly coordinated and dangerous. Any Jack or any Nine in Pro's range would now have a full house. Pro has check-called every street, which could signify a slow-played monster or a drawing hand that may have improved. By checking behind on the river, I ensure a showdown with a hand that has significant showdown value without risking a check-raise from a superior hand like a full house or a better two-pair (AQ).

I hold Pocket Fives (5c 5s) on the Button/Small Blind. In Heads-Up play, any pocket pair is a strong starting hand, typically ahead of an opponent's random range. My strategy from the button has consistently been a minimum raise to 200, which has been effective in taking the initiative or getting called by the Big Blind who tends to play passively post-flop. I will raise to 200 to build the pot, deny free equity to the Big Blind, and maintain consistent aggressive play.

I am in the Big Blind with Q4o. Pro has raised to 200 from the Small Blind/Button. I need to call 100 more into a pot that will be 400. This is a 3:1 price. While Q4o is a weak hand, in heads-up play, the pot odds often justify a defense against small raises to see a flop, especially since I have position post-flop in some structures, though here the SB is the button. However, looking at the previous hands, Pro has been playing quite tight-aggressively and I've been folding a lot. Given the stack sizes and the relatively weak nature of Q4o, I will fold to maintain my stack for better spots, as Q4o plays poorly post-flop when out of position. Actually, looking at the tournament status, I am the BB and Pro is the Button/SB. I am getting 3:1, but Q4o is too weak to realize equity effectively. I will fold.

I am on the button with 82o, which is a very weak hand. While I have seen Pro fold to several pre-flop raises recently, 82o is near the bottom of my range and doesn't warrant a steal attempt or a call. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the Button (SB) with Q7o, which is a playable hand in Heads-Up No-Limit Hold'em (top ~52% of hands). My opponent, Flash, has shown a tendency to defend his Big Blind by calling min-raises but plays relatively passively post-flop. Since I will have position throughout the hand, raising allows me to take the initiative and potentially take down the pot pre-flop or navigate effectively post-flop. I have been using a min-raise strategy (200) for my opens, and I will continue that here.

With J7o in the Big Blind, I am facing a minimum raise (200 chips) from the Button. I need to call 100 more to see a flop with a total pot of 400, which gives me 3:1 pot odds. In heads-up play, J7o is a reasonable hand to defend against a wide button opening range, especially given the favorable price.

On a flop of Tc 8s 2d, I have Jack-high and a gutshot straight draw (any 9 gives me a straight draw, though not an open-ended one). Standard procedure in heads-up play as the Big Blind is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. Leading here wouldn't accomplish much against a wide button opening range, and checking allows me to see a bet size before deciding whether to continue with my marginal equity.

I hold Q7o on a Tc 8s 2d rainbow board. The board is relatively dry, hitting a portion of the opponent's calling range (Tx, 8x, straight draws like J9, 97). As the pre-flop aggressor in position, I have the option to c-bet, but Q7 has limited equity when called and doesn't block many value hands. Checking back allows me to realize my equity (6 outs to a pair) for free, keeps the pot small, and avoids getting check-raised or floating a bet into a range that often connects with a Ten or Eight. My hand has some showdown value against total air, and I can re-evaluate on the turn.

I am in the Big Blind and the action is on me after a checked flop. The board is Tc 8s 2d 6h. I currently have Jack-high with a gutshot straight draw (a 9 gives me a straight). Pro checked back on the flop, which often indicates a weak range or a marginal hand that didn't want to build a pot. By checking here, I can potentially see a free river card to hit my straight or simply reach a cheap showdown if Pro also checks behind. Leading out as a bluff is an option, but checking is a safer way to realize my equity.

On the turn, the board is Tc 8s 2d 6h. I have Q-high and have picked up a gutshot straight draw (needing a 9). The opponent has checked to me twice, showing passivity or weakness. While a delayed c-bet could fold out better high cards (A-high/K-high), it likely won't fold out any pair (Tx, 8x, 6x) which connects well with this board. Checking back allows me to realize my equity (4 outs to the straight, plus 6 outs to a pair) for free without facing the risk of a check-raise. I will take the free card to try and improve on the river.

I have Jack-high on a board of Tc 8s 2d 6h 4c. Both the flop and turn were checked through, which suggests Pro has a weak or marginal hand. While I could attempt a bluff to take down the small pot, checking is the most prudent play. If Pro checks behind, I might occasionally win against a lower high card hand, and if Pro bets, I can easily fold without losing more chips.

I hold Q7o (Queen-high) on a T-8-2-6-4 board. The action has gone check-check on the flop and turn. My opponent (Flash) has checked to me on the river. If they bet, I will have to evaluate the sizing, but leading into the aggressor on this board texture makes little sense for my specific holding.

I hold Top Pair with a weak kicker (A4o) on a board of A-7-2-J. After betting the flop and getting called, the turn Jack introduces some connectivity (potential straight draws, second pair for Jx hands) and a flush draw. While I still likely have the best hand, my kicker is vulnerable, and I am unlikely to get three streets of value from worse hands like 7x or underpairs. Betting might isolate me against better Aces or two-pair hands. Checking back allows me to exercise pot control, induces bluffs from the opponent on the river, and allows me to comfortably call a reasonable river bet or value bet if checked to again. This keeps the opponent's range wide.

I have a pair of deuces on a board of A-J-8-7-2. The turn went check-check after I called a small flop bet from Pro. Pro's check on the turn suggests they might have a weak made hand they are trying to take to showdown or a missed draw. With bottom pair, I have very little showdown value, but I don't see much merit in betting as a bluff because hands that beat me (7s, 8s, Jacks, or weak Aces) are unlikely to fold for a single river bet after checking the turn. Checking is the most prudent play to see a cheap showdown or evaluate if Pro decides to bet.

I hold Top Pair (Aces) with a weak kicker (4). The pot is 700. After betting the flop and checking back the turn, the river is an 8d. The board is A-7-2-J-8. My opponent checks to me. My hand has significant showdown value, beating any pair of Jacks, 8s, 7s, or pocket pairs. However, my kicker is very weak, meaning I lose to almost any other Ace (A5+). Betting for value is thin because I only target Jx or sticky 7x hands, while opening myself up to being called by better Aces or check-raised by two pair. Since my opponent has been passive, checking back ensures I realize my equity against his second-best hands and avoid losing value to better Aces that also took a pot-control line. The risk of value-betting effectively (being called only by better, or folding out worse) outweighs the potential reward.

I have been dealt pocket deuces on the button. While it is the lowest possible pocket pair, any pair has significant value in heads-up play, often being a favorite against two overcards. I have a substantial chip lead and intend to maintain pressure by raising. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here to either take down the blinds or see a flop with the initiative.

Opponent 'Flash' is opening very frequently (approx 3x raise size) from the button. My hand, A7o, is well above the median hand strength in a heads-up scenario and has roughly 55-60% equity against a wide opening range. The pot odds are favorable (calling 200 to win a total pot of 600, requiring 25% equity). Folding would be too tight given the opponent's aggressive frequency. A 3-bet is an option but bloats the pot out of position with a marginal made hand. Calling allows me to keep the opponent's range wide and evaluate the flop, exploiting the fact that Flash has a high c-bet frequency on later streets.

I hold Ace-high (Ah) with a weak backdoor flush draw on a board of Kd-Jc-8d. This texture heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range, which likely contains many Kings, Jacks, and broadway draws. My opponent, Flash, has shown a high frequency of continuation betting (c-betting) when checked to. Leading out (donk betting) would likely only get called by better hands or fold out hands I am beating. Checking is the standard play here to keep the opponent's range wide, induce bluffs, or potentially see a free turn if they check back.

On a flop of King-Jack-Eight, my pocket deuces have lost almost all their showdown value against the range of hands the opponent would call with pre-flop. This board is quite heavy with high cards and potential draws (like a diamond flush draw or straight draws), making it likely that the opponent has connected in some way or has overcards with significant equity. While I was the pre-flop aggressor, betting here into a board that hits the Big Blind's calling range so well is risky with only a small pair. By checking back, I control the size of the pot and get to see a turn card for free, allowing me to evaluate the action further without overcommitting with a weak hand.

On the turn, the board is [Kd Jc 8d 3c]. The opponent checked back the flop, which caps their range, suggesting they likely lack a strong King or a set, as those hands usually bet for value on this draw-heavy texture. My hand is Ace-high (A7), which has reasonable showdown value against missed draws or weaker broadway hands. Betting (probing) is an option to fold out small pairs (22-66) or better Ace-highs, but it risks getting called by weak Jacks, Kings, or draws, bloating the pot out of position with a marginal hand. Checking allows me to control the pot size and attempt to get to showdown cheaply. If the opponent checks back again, I have a decent chance of winning or seeing a river to potentially improve or catch a bluff. Therefore, checking is the most standard and low-variance line.

The turn card is a 3 of clubs, which doesn't significantly change the board texture. Since I checked back on the flop to control the pot with my pocket deuces, and the opponent has checked again on the turn, I am inclined to check behind once more. My hand has very little showdown value against the opponent's calling range, but betting here likely won't force out any better hands like a J, 8, or even a strong draw. Checking allows me to see the river for free and potentially win at showdown if the opponent is holding missed overcards or a lower draw.

The opponent has checked back both the flop and the turn, signaling a capped range likely consisting of weak pairs or missed draws. My hand, Ace-high, has showdown value against the missed draws (diamonds and clubs) and weaker high cards. Betting is unlikely to get value from worse hands and turning my hand into a bluff might not fold out small pairs that the opponent has pot-controlled. Checking allows me to realize my equity at showdown for free or fold comfortably if the opponent decides to bluff-bet the river, avoiding unnecessary chip loss in a small pot.

I have pocket deuces, which is a small pair. The board has three overcards (K, J, 8) and two low cards (3, 5). Both the opponent and I have checked on the flop and turn. The opponent has now checked the river as well. In heads-up play, a small pair has significant showdown value against missed draws and non-paired hands like Ace-high. Betting here is unlikely to get a better hand to fold, nor is it likely to be called by a worse hand. Therefore, checking back to see a free showdown is the most prudent play.

I hold KcQd on the Button (SB), which is a premium hand in heads-up play. With 56 BBs, I want to build the pot and seize the initiative. My standard play in this tournament has been a min-raise to 200, which Flash often calls. This sizing allows me to play a pot in position with a strong range advantage. There is no reason to deviate from the standard aggressive opening here.

I am holding King-Jack of spades, a very strong starting hand in heads-up play. My opponent has raised to 200 from the small blind. I have a significant chip lead and want to apply pressure while building a pot with a hand that has high equity. I will 3-bet to 600 to take control of the hand and potentially win the pot pre-flop or set up a favorable post-flop situation.

I hold KcQd, a premium hand in heads-up play, and have position post-flop. Flash has 3-bet to 600. Folding is far too weak given the strength of my hand. 4-betting risks isolation against a stronger range (AK, QQ+) or facing a shove for my tournament life. Calling is the most prudent play: I get good pot odds (calling 400 to play a pot of 1200), I retain position, and I keep Flash's range wider. I will call the 3-bet and evaluate the flop.

I was the aggressor pre-flop with a 3-bet holding King-Jack of spades. The flop comes Queen-high with a club draw. While I haven't hit a pair, the Queen is a card that favors my perceived 3-betting range (which includes AA, KK, QQ, and AQ). I have two overcards to the middle of the board and a backdoor spade flush draw. I will lead out with a continuation bet of 400 (one-third of the pot) to maintain initiative, represent the Queen, and fold out weaker hands or under-pairs in the opponent's range.

I have flopped Top Pair with a strong Kicker (Kings) on a board of 7c 4c Qs. In a 3-bet pot, this is a very strong hand. Flash has continued with a small bet of 400 into 1200 (1/3 pot). This sizing is standard for a wide range of hands including value (AQ, KK, AA) and bluffs/semi-bluffs (flush draws, AK, straight draws). Folding is out of the question. Raising would likely isolate me against hands that beat me (AQ+) or strong draws, potentially folding out the bluffs I want to keep in the pot. Calling allows me to control the pot size, keep my opponent's range wide, and use my position to evaluate the turn. I also hold the Kc, which blocks some flush draws or gives me backdoor equity. The price is excellent.

The turn card is the deuce of clubs, completing the flush draw that was present on the flop. While I was the aggressor pre-flop and on the flop, this board texture is now quite dangerous. I do not have a club in my hand, meaning I have zero flush equity. Pro called my pre-flop 3-bet and my flop continuation bet, suggesting they have a piece of this board or a draw. If I continue betting here, I am essentially turning my King-high into a total bluff against a range that likely contains many clubs or a Queen. I will check to see how Pro responds to the flush-completing card and try to control the size of the pot, potentially seeking a cheap showdown if they check behind, or folding if they bet large.

On the turn, the board is [7c 4c Qs 2c], completing the flush draw. I hold Top Pair (Queens) with a King kicker and the King of Clubs (Kc), giving me a redraw to the second nut flush. Flash checks to me. Betting here accomplishes little: worse hands likely fold, and better hands (flushes, sets, AQ) continue or raise. I can re-evaluate on the river.

The board now shows four clubs, and I do not hold a club in my hand, meaning I have no flush equity. My King-high has almost no showdown value in this spot, as the opponent likely called the pre-flop 3-bet and flop continuation bet with a hand that contains a pair or a draw. Having checked the turn, I have surrendered initiative. Betting here would be a pure bluff on a very wet board where the opponent only needs a single club to call. I will check and hope for a cheap showdown, though I expect to fold if faced with any significant bet.

The river brings the 9c, putting four clubs on the board (7c 4c Qs 2c 9c). I hold the Kc, giving me the King-high flush, which is the second nuts. The only card that beats me is the Ace of Clubs. Given the density of Aces in a 3-bet range and the risk of value-owning myself against the nut flush, checking back is the prudent play to realize my equity and win the pot against all non-Ac holdings without risking more chips.

I am on the button with Ace-Seven offsuit, which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. My strategy throughout this match has been to raise to 300 from the small blind to put pressure on the opponent and capitalize on my positional advantage. I hold Pocket Deuces (2c 2d) in the Big Blind. My opponent, Flash, has raised to 300 (3BB) from the Button. Folding a pocket pair heads-up for a single raise is too tight, and 3-betting turns the hand into a bluff catcher or folds out hands I flip against. A call is the most prudent action.

I have flopped a set of deuces on an A-5-2 board, which is a very strong hand. Being out of position against the pre-flop raiser, the standard and most effective line is to check. This gives my opponent, who likely connects with the Ace or holds a draw, the opportunity to make a continuation bet. Leading out would deny him the chance to bluff or bet a weaker Ace. I intend to check-raise or check-call depending on the size of his bet, but checking is the clear first step.

I have top pair with an Ace on a relatively dry board of A-5-2. My opponent checked to me after calling pre-flop. I will place a continuation bet to extract value from weaker pairs (like 5x or pocket pairs) and to protect against potential draws (like 3-4 or heart draws). A bet of 300 into a 600 pot is a standard sized value bet in this situation.

I have flopped bottom set on an A-5-2 board with a flush draw. My opponent has bet half-pot (300 into 600). The Ace hits the opponent's opening range hard, but he is also an aggressive player capable of multi-street bluffs (as seen in Hand #18 and #83). Raising now risks folding out his air or semi-bluffs that might barrel the turn. Because I unblock his Ax hands and bluffs, and given his aggressive history, calling is the best line to extract value on later streets or let him hang himself. I need to protect against the flush draw, but his aggression makes slow-playing slightly more profitable here to induce a turn bet.

I hold bottom set (22) on a board of 2h-5s-Ah-8d. The turn card (8d) is relatively safe, though it brings a second diamond and 3-4 is now a completed straight (unlikely holding). Leading out (donk betting) would likely fold out his air and define my hand too early. I intend to check-raise if he bets to build the pot and charge draws, or check-call if the sizing suggests he is light.

I have top pair with an Ace on a dry board of A-5-2-8. Pro checked to me for the second time after calling my flop continuation bet. This suggests Pro has a marginal hand like a weaker Ace, a pocket pair, or a straight draw (3-4). I will bet 600 into the 1200 pot to extract value from these hands and to protect my lead. If Pro has improved with an 8, I still likely have the best hand with my pair of Aces.

I hold a set of Deuces (bottom set) on an A-5-2-8 board. My opponent has double-barreled for half pot. Based on previous hands (e.g., #15, #24, #54, #83), this opponent has a high tendency to fold to aggression (raises) on the turn and flop. Raising now would likely force him to fold his bluffs and weak semi-bluffs, and perhaps even some weaker Ax hands, denying me value. By calling, I keep his entire range in the pot, including bluffs that are drawing dead and value hands that might bet the river or call a river lead. While the flush draw is present, the risk of a flush completing is outweighed by the risk of blowing the opponent off the hand given his history of folding to raises. I am way ahead of his range and will call to extract maximum value on the river.

I hold a Full House (Deuces full of Aces) on a board of 2h-5s-Ah-8d-Ac. The river Ace is an interesting card; it improves any Ax hand to at least Trip Aces, but it also gives boats to A5 and A8, which would beat me (Aces full of Fives/Eights > Deuces full of Aces). If I lead out now, I fold out his missed draws and bluffs, and potentially scare off weaker Aces. By checking, I allow him to value bet his Trips (thinking they are best) or fire a third barrel as a bluff. Given his aggressive profile, checking to induce a bet is the most profitable line. I plan to call or raise depending on the sizing, but the first action must be a check.

On the river, the second Ace pairs the board, giving me trip Aces. However, my kicker is only a Seven. Pro has called two streets of betting already, which suggests they likely have a piece of the board or a drawing hand. If Pro has an Ace, they almost certainly have a better kicker (AK, AQ, AJ, AT, A9) or we are chopping (A6-A2). If Pro has a pocket pair or an Eight, they are unlikely to call another bet. By checking back, I ensure I reach showdown and realize my equity without risking a check-raise or losing more chips to a superior Ace or a full house (like A8, A5, or A2).

I hold Js9s on the Button (SB). This is a strong suited connector in heads-up play, well above the median hand strength. I am in the Big Blind with Qc 5c, a suited hand with some post-flop potential. Pro raised to 200 from the Small Blind/Button. On this dry flop of 9h 3c 2s, my hand (Queen-high with a backdoor club draw) has not connected. In heads-up play, the Big Blind acts first post-flop. I hold Top Pair (Nines) with a Jack kicker on a very dry, rainbow board (9h-3c-2s). The board is static with no immediate flush draws, meaning giving a free card is not dangerous. I will check back to keep the pot manageable and look to call or value bet on later streets.

On the turn 6c, my hand equity has significantly improved. I now hold a flush draw and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 4). I hold Top Pair (Nines) with a Jack kicker. After checking back the flop to control the pot and induce action, the opponent has led into me on the turn for 250 into a pot of 400. Raising would likely only get action from hands that beat me (sets, two pair, better kickers) and fold out the bluffs I want to keep in the pot. On the river, I have improved to a pair of fives. The board is 9-3-2-6-5. While I have some showdown value, it is relatively weak. I hold Top Pair (Nines) with a Jack kicker. The board runout (9-3-2-6-5) is somewhat dangerous as any 4 makes a straight. The opponent led the turn and checked the river. I am on the button with A4o, which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play. My opponent, Pro, has a stack of 82 BBs and has shown a tendency to fold to button raises in previous hands. I am in the Big Blind with 7h 3s, which is an extremely weak hand (7-3 offsuit). The opponent, Flash, has raised to 300 chips (3 big blinds) from the Button/Small Blind. I hold Queen-Six suited (Q6s) on the button (SB). In heads-up play, Q6s is a strong enough hand to open-raise for value and to steal the blinds. I am in the Big Blind holding 7c 8d. Pro has raised to 200 from the Button. To call, I only need to contribute 100 more chips into a pot that currently stands at 300. On this flop of Jc-6c-2d, I have 8-high with no direct pair or draw. As the Big Blind, I have checked to the raiser (Pro). Pro has also checked back. I hold middle pair (Sixes) with a Queen kicker on a J-6-2 two-tone board. While I have showdown value, my hand is vulnerable to overcards and draws. This is a standard pot control spot for a marginal made hand in position.

The turn brings a second six, pairing the board. I currently hold 8-high with no draws. Pro checked back on the flop, which often indicates a capped or marginal range. However, 8-high has almost no showdown value and very little equity. On the Flop, I checked back with middle pair (Sixes) for pot control. The Turn card is the Six of Spades, improving my hand to Three of a Kind (Trips). I am currently holding 8-high on a board of Jc-6c-2d-6s. After checking the flop and turn, Pro has bet 200 into a 400 pot. My hand has no showdown value and no draws to improve to a winning hand. I am on the button with T6o. In a heads-up situation, T6o is a hand with some playability and typically merits a raise to put pressure on the big blind. Opponent raises to 300 (3BB) from the button. I am in the Big Blind with 7d 5d, a suited one-gapper. We are playing Heads-Up with deep stacks (83BB effective). I hold 7d5d on a board of Ac 3d 4c. This gives me a double gutshot straight draw (a 2 makes A-2-3-4-5, a 6 makes 3-4-5-6-7), totaling 8 outs. I raised pre-flop with T6o and have the initiative. The flop comes Ac-3d-4c, which is a very strong board for my perceived range as the pre-flop aggressor. Pro checked, which is expected. By betting here, I am representing an Ace or a strong draw. Given Pro's tendency to fold to aggression when they haven't connected with the board, a half-pot continuation bet should be effective at taking down the pot immediately.

I hold a double gutshot straight draw (needs a 2 or 6) on an [Ac 3d 4c] board, giving me 8 outs. The opponent has bet 300 into a pot of 600, offering me 3:1 pot odds (25% equity required to call). My equity with 8 outs is approximately 16-17% to hit on the turn and ~32% by the river. Given the implied odds if I hit my straight and the fact that 3:1 is a reasonable price to see the next card, folding would be too tight. Raising is risky as the Ace favors the opponent's range. Calling is the most positive EV play.

I hold 7d5d on a board of [Ac 3d 4c Ks]. The turn card (Ks) is a high card that favors the pre-flop aggressor's range (Ax, Kx, broadways) significantly more than my calling range. If they check back, I realize my equity for free. If they bet, I will evaluate the pot odds versus my implied odds. Therefore, I will check.

On the turn, the King of Spades is an interesting card. It's a second overcard to the board. However, Pro called my flop continuation bet on an Ace-high board. Since I only have Ten-high with no significant draw, and my flop bet failed to fold the opponent, it is highly likely they have an Ace or at least a pair they are unwilling to part with. Attempting a second barrel against a sticky opponent on this board texture is risky and could lead to a significant loss of chips. I hold 7d5d on a board of Ac 3d 4c Ks 8h. I missed my double gutshot straight draw and have 7-high, which has zero showdown value. The opponent raised pre-flop, c-bet the Ace-high flop, and checked back the King turn. While my hand loses to everything, including the opponent's bluffs (like Q-high), bluffing into a board containing an Ace and a King is dangerous. The opponent's range likely contains many pairs that will call a river bet (Ax, Kx, bottom pairs). Trying to fold out better hands is low probability and high risk. The most prudent play is to check and concede the pot to preserve my stack.

On the river, the board is Ac-3d-4c-Ks-8h. I have Ten-high. Pro called my flop continuation bet, which strongly suggests they have a piece of the board, likely an Ace, a small pair, or a draw. After the turn went check-check, Pro has checked the river as well. While Pro's check on the river could indicate a missed draw or a weak hand, I have zero showdown value with Ten-high. However, Pro has shown a tendency to be sticky with pairs in previous hands.
